feedback.text: The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.

paragraphs.text: Digital Fairness Act
Call for evidence – Feedback
Unfair online practices related to pricing should be regulated restrictively, especially those suggesting rebates and savings, as fake discounts have become ubiquitous in e-commerce.
Price comparisons based on mere price recommendations (RRP), or other hypothetical price calculations, should no longer be legally privileged over ads based on real price reductions.

The use of recommended retail prices (RRP) in marketing should be specified at EU level, and transparency increased. The burden of proof should always be with the advertiser, not the consumer. Brands that opt for RRPs should publish them online.

Regulation should anticipate future developments (creative marketing, online advertisement, AI) and therefore make use of general clauses rather than case-by-case legislation, to prevent circumvention of rules.

Discriminatory practices, such as personalised pricing, should be banned due to their inherent non-transparency. Instead, retailers should have to make their pricing as transparent as possible. Full transparency limits the requirement for legal disputes and increasing regulation.
Compliance and enforcement should be improved through more transparency, accessibility and consumer information, making use of AI tools and online databases.

The Digital Fairness Act is meant to address “unfair practices related to the price (e.g. … percentage/value discounts that mislead the consumer as to the nature of the promotion)”, according to the call for evidence document, Ares(2025)5829481.
Please allow me to comment on this matter as an interested EU citizen from Germany.

Not only in brick-and-mortar retail but even more so online, pricing should comply with the principles of price transparency as laid down in the Price Indication Directive (Directive 98/6/EC). Price clarity and price truth („Preisklarheit und
Preiswahrheit" in German regulation, PAngV) must apply to every aspect of pricing, including comparative prices, price recommendations and calculations.

Unfortunately, in this regard, it seems to me that the online world lags far behind the brick-and-mortar world. While there are comprehensible reasons for that, I agree that online fairness needs a specific focus as to how the general rules of fair play can be enforced in a far more fragmented, more dynamic and short-lived environment without over-regulation and paralysation. Whether this requires new regulation or can be implemented in existing policies can remain open, in my view.

As a shopper, I strongly oppose personalised pricing. It is non-transparent, it is undemocratic, and it ignores the fact that consumers can never be expected to negotiate on a level playing field with sellers. While it may sound attractive to get a 'better' price individually, this will only result in the general default prices going up, especially in increasingly mature and oligopolistic retail markets. If this is nevertheless desired, it should be regulated in a smart, flexible and enforceable manner, increasing transparency. (E.g. the requirement to indicate the highest and lowest actual prices that the retailers has effectively charged so far, or a maximum price.)

'Fairness' (as in Digital Fairness Act) is always a matter of judgement. Given the dynamic nature of the internet, including AI, and the fact that price marketing is subject to the particular creativity of the ad industry, I would urge that (online) pricing be regulated not only comprehensively, but also in a future-proof and flexible manner. Flexibility means that overly detailed case-by-case regulations should be avoided and that instead, general clauses with a blacklist of undesirable methods should be established or expanded. This prevents marketing and the advertising industry from creatively circumventing standards, and counteracts a constant race to keep up, thus creating an excessive proliferation of rules. This should be a learning from the latest addition to the Price Indication Directive, Article 6a; the 30+ day period specified there (for price reductions, the lowest reference price of the previous 30 days or more must be indicated) has caused legal uncertainty in countries such as Germany, and tied up judicial capacity, right up to the ECJ (see ECJ C-330/23). As a result, the rule is increasingly being circumvented, e.g. by using comparisons with Recommended Retail Prices (RRP, UVP, see more below) instead of actual price reductions.

That said, with case-by-case legislation similar to Article 6a, the regulator would always be playing catch-up with the endless creativity of online marketing. Marketers are permanently inventing new ways of price advertising, e.g. self-calculated 'average prices' (as used by a large US online retailer), or comparisons with unspecified 'standard' pack sizes (a European food discount retailer), or using various store logos on the identical online shop to differentiate prices depending on search tools used (a Dutch online pharmacy). From the outset, flexible and forward-looking regulation should therefore include consideration of more complex circumvention scenarios and alternatives, also anticipating future AI development, e.g. when AI agents increasingly interact with each other.
Regulations should always take into account the realistic options of legal enforcement for consumers, especially in EU and international e-commerce.

Realistically, a discriminatory measure which is not even apparent to me (such as personalised pricing), or for which I as a consumer would have the burden of proof, will rarely be sanctioned effectively. Consumer information and transparency should be enforced using AI. The more transparency, the less need for actual law enforcement and additional regulation.

The EU Commission should finally regulate the use of Recommended Retail Prices (RRP) in consumer communications. It would help reduce falsely inflated savings promotions online and offline. The RRP, which traditionally is mostly codified at national levels, in fact does not represent a real price but a (more or less) seriously calculated conception of a brand manufacturer. Unfortunately, it is increasingly being used to replace genuine price reductions in retail ads, circumventing the stricter and more consumer-friendly provisions of the Price Indication Directive (which includes elements such as the aforementioned Article 6a or the reversal of the burden of proof).

From my perspective as a consumer, there is little to no interest in retailers advertising with RRP. Real retailers should use real prices – their own or those of others – as they are freely permitted to do. There is no consumer benefit in referencing an external price that neither the advertiser nor any competitor has charged; if indeed there is such a competitor, then the advertiser is explicitly allowed by EU competition law to name the respective retailer, while taking the legal consequences for his ad to be correct.

If a manufacturer announces RRPs – which it is free but not required to do – it should be required to publish these RRPs online so that shoppers, consumer protection bodies and competitors have full transparency.

Unfortunately, outdated RRPs are ubiquitous especially on the internet, e.g. to promote technical goods like smartphones or laptops or other products with a short innovation cycle; but collecting legal evidence is difficult for consumers and their representatives, due to a general lack of transparency and a collusion of interests: Retailers and brand suppliers both have a tacit common interest in the highest possible RRP. Finally, determining whether or not an RRP is 'realistic' and 'seriously calculated' is more pointless than ever in the internet era when you can book the same flight to Mallorca for 10 euros or for 1,000 euros.

In any case, generally speaking, it is an unwanted contradiction (and unfair) that ads promoting savings on a mere price suggestion are less strictly regulated than advertising with a genuine price reduction, i.e. a saving that is based on a price that was actually charged by the retailer (Article 6a etc).
Setting limits on overly creative pricing would help promoting seriousness in the virtual world.
Thank you.
